1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.

13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).

To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.

They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.

92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}
79	
80	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
82	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
83	  \mathlibok
84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
85	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
87	  \[
88	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.
89	  \]
90	\end{lemma}
91	
92	\begin{lemma}[Long exact homology sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes]
93	  \label{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
94	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁,
95	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₂,
96	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₃,
97	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ}
98	  \mathlibok
99	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
100	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be an abelian category and let
101	  \[
102	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
103	      \longrightarrow 0
104	  \]
105	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
106	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
107	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)
108	  which is short exact (degreewise mono, epi and exact in the middle). Then for every
109	  degree \(n\) there is a connecting morphism
110	  \[
111	    \delta^n \;:\; H^n(S.X_3) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
112	  \]
113	  (the Mathlib map \texttt{ShortComplex.ShortExact.\(\delta\)}), and these assemble
114	  into a long exact sequence of homology objects in \(\mathcal{C}\)
115	  \[
116	    \cdots \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_2) \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_3)
117	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^n\ } H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
118	      \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_2) \longrightarrow \cdots .
119	  \]
120	  This is the complex-level homology long exact sequence and its connecting
121	  homomorphism. The derived-functor long exact sequence used in the sequel is
122	  constructed from this complex-level result via a horseshoe lift; see
123	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}.
124	\end{lemma}
125	
126	\section{Right-acyclic objects}
127	
128	\begin{definition}[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
129	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
130	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
131	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
132	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition
133	  % \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157, items 3--4) and Lemma
134	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C, part 2)
135	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5264--5269 and
136	  % L5594--5617).
137	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
138	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
139	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
140	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
141	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
142	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
143	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
144	  % between abelian categories and assume
145	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
146	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
147	  % \begin{enumerate}
148	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
149	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
150	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
151	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
152	  % \end{enumerate}"
153	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
154	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
155	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
156	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
157	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
158	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
159	  \[
160	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
161	  \]
162	  Equivalently, writing the condition with the index shifted, \((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\)
163	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
164	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
165	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
166	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
167	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
168	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
169	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.
170	
171	  \emph{Remark (alternative quantifier shapes).} Two equivalent formulations are
172	  available: ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\)'' or the index-shifted
173	  ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\)''; the latter avoids the
174	  inequality side-condition and aligns with the vanishing statement of
175	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}.
176	\end{definition}
177	
178	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
179	
180	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
181	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
182	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
183	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
184	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
185	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
186	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
187	lift.
188	
189	\begin{lemma}[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
190	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
191	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
192	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
193	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
194	  \[
195	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
196	  \]
197	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
198	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
199	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
200	  \[
201	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
202	  \]
203	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
204	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
205	  \[
206	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
207	      \longrightarrow 0
208	  \]
209	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
210	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
211	\end{lemma}
212	
213	\begin{proof}
214	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
215	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma; see e.g.\ Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
216	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8 (stated there for projective resolutions; the
217	  injective-resolution form is the dual). Choose injective resolutions
218	  \(A \to I_A^\bullet\) and \(C \to I_C^\bullet\), which exist because \(\mathcal{A}\)
219	  has enough injectives. Set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) in each degree; this is
220	  injective, being a finite direct sum of injectives. The maps
221	  \(I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and \(I_B^n \to I_C^n\) are the canonical coproduct inclusion
222	  and product projection, so each degree is split exact by construction. It remains
223	  to build the augmentation \(B \to I_B^0\) and the differentials
224	  \(I_B^n \to I_B^{n+1}\) making \(I_B^\bullet\) a resolution of \(B\) and the two
225	  vertical families chain maps. One proceeds degree by degree: at each stage the
226	  required lift exists because the relevant target summand \(I_A^{\bullet}\) is
227	  injective and the relevant source map is a monomorphism, so the universal lifting
228	  property of injectives produces the missing component; the diagonal differential of
229	  \(I_B^\bullet\) is then assembled from the differentials of \(I_A^\bullet\),
230	  \(I_C^\bullet\) and these lifts. Exactness of the resulting \(I_B^\bullet\) (that it
231	  resolves \(B\)) follows from the long exact homology sequence
232	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to the degreewise-split short
233	  exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet \to 0\): the
234	  outer complexes are acyclic resolutions, so the connecting maps force
235	  \(I_B^\bullet\) to be acyclic in positive degrees and to have \(H^0 = B\).
236	\end{proof}
237	
238	\section{The dimension-shift step}
239	
240	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
241	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
242	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
243	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
244	complex-level homology long exact sequence
245	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
246	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We isolate the result as the kernel
247	sub-lemma below.
248	
249	\begin{lemma}[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
250	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
251	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
252	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
253	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
254	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
255	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
256	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
257	  % between abelian categories and assume
258	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
259	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
260	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
261	  % \begin{enumerate}
262	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
263	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
264	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
265	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
266	  % \end{enumerate}
267	  % In each of the three cases
268	  % $$
269	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
270	  % $$
271	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
272	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
273	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
274	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
275	  \[
276	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
277	  \]
278	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
279	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then:
280	  \begin{enumerate}
281	    \item the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of right-derived functors
282	      furnish isomorphisms
283	      \[
284	        (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
285	        \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1;
286	      \]
287	    \item in the lowest degree there is an isomorphism
288	      \[
289	        (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
290	      \]
291	      i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by
292	      the surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
293	  \end{enumerate}
294	\end{lemma}
295	
296	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
297	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
298	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
299	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
300	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
301	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
302	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
303	\begin{proof}
304	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
305	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
306	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
307	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
308	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
309	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
310	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
311	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
312	
313	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
314	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
315	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
316	  complexes
317	  \[
318	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
319	  \]
320	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
321	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
322	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
323	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
324	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
325	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
326	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
327	  \[
328	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
329	      \longrightarrow 0
330	  \]
331	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
332	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
333	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
334	  \[
335	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
336	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
337	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
338	  \]
339	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
340	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
341	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
342	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
343	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
344	  sequence of right-derived functors
345	  \[
346	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
347	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
348	  \]
349	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
350	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
351	  \[
352	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
353	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
354	  \]
355	
356	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
357	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
358	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
359	  \[
360	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
361	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
362	  \]
363	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
364	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism; this is part (1).
365	  In the lowest degree the segment
366	  \[
367	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A)
368	      \to \underbrace{(R^1 G)(J)}_{=0}
369	  \]
370	  is exact with vanishing right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is
371	  the image of \(G(J) \to G(Z)\); hence \(\delta^0\) identifies
372	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr)\) with \((R^1 G)(A)\), which is
373	  part (2).
374	\end{proof}

409	  lemma) and Tag 05TA (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
410	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
411	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
412	  Let
413	  \[
414	    0 \longrightarrow A
415	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
416	      \longrightarrow \cdots

447	\begin{proof}
448	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
449	    lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
450	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
451	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
452	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, with the long exact sequence at each
453	  step supplied by the kernel Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}. Decompose the
454	  resolution into the
455	  short exact sequences obtained from its cosyzygies. Put \(Z^0 := A\) and, for
456	  \(m \geq 1\), let \(Z^m := \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr) =
457	  \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr)\) be the \(m\)-th cosyzygy; exactness
458	  of the resolution gives, for every \(m \geq 0\), a short exact sequence
459	  \[
460	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
461	      \longrightarrow 0, \tag{$S_m$}
462	  \]
463	  with \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\) and
464	  the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis.
465	
466	  \emph{Base degrees.} Applying the left-exact functor \(G\) to the resolution and
467	  using Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self} (\(R^0 G \cong G\)), the complex
468	  \(G(J^\bullet)\) has, in degrees \(0\) and \(1\),
469	  \[
470	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) = \ker\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)\bigr) \cong G(A)
471	      = (R^0 G)(A),
472	  \]
473	  the last identification because \(G\) left exact carries
474	  \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\) to a left-exact sequence
475	  \(0 \to G(A) \to G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) and \(Z^1 \hookrightarrow J^1\) makes
476	  \(\ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)) = G(A)\). For degree one,
477	  the dimension-shift Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to the
478	  sequence \((S_0)\), \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\), gives
479	  \[
480	    (R^1 G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\bigr)
481	      \;\cong\; H^1\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
482	  \]
483	  the second isomorphism because \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1) \hookrightarrow G(J^1)\) and the
484	  image of \(G(Z^1)\) in \(G(J^1)\) is exactly \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2))\) (again by
485	  left-exactness of \(G\) applied to \((S_1)\)), so the cokernel of
486	  \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) coincides with \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2)) /
487	  \operatorname{im}(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^1(G(J^\bullet))\).
488	
489	  \emph{Induction (the shift).} For \(n \geq 1\), iterate the connecting
490	  isomorphism. The sequences \((S_0), (S_1), \ldots, (S_{n-1})\) all have acyclic
491	  middle terms, so Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(1) yields, for each
492	  \(1 \le m \le n-1\) and each \(k \geq 1\),
493	  \[
494	    (R^{k} G)(Z^{m+1}) \;\cong\; (R^{k+1} G)(Z^{m}),
495	  \]
496	  and applying part (1) to \((S_0)\) gives
497	  \((R^{k} G)(Z^{1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) for \(k \geq 1\). Composing these
498	  isomorphisms down the staircase
499	  \[
500	    (R^n G)(A)
501	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
502	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
503	      \;\cong\; \cdots
504	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1}),
505	  \]
506	  reduces the computation of \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor, which
507	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to \((S_{n-1})\),
508	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), evaluates as
509	  \[
510	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
511	  \]
512	  Finally, as in the degree-one base case, left-exactness of \(G\) applied to
513	  \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\), identifies the inclusion
514	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) with \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\), whence
515	  \[
516	    \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr)
517	      \;\cong\;
518	      \frac{\ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr)}
519	           {\operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr)}
520	      \;=\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr).
521	  \]
522	  Combining the staircase with this last identification gives
523	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for all \(n \geq 1\); together with the
524	  base degrees this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
525	
526	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the kernel
527	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
528	  horseshoe lift (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) and the complex-level
529	  homology long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) --- on
530	  the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\)
531	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). The injective objects supplied
532	  by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
533	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
534	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
535	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
536	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
537	\end{proof}

10	# Čech computation of the higher direct images `Rⁱ f_*` (unconditional)
11	
12	This file constructs the higher derived direct images `Rⁱ f_* F` for `i ≥ 1`
13	**without appealing to injective resolutions** in the category of sheaves of
14	modules. The companion `Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean` defines `Rⁱ f_*` as a
15	right derived functor, which requires the ambient category of `O_X`-modules to
16	have enough injectives — a property not currently available for sheaves of
17	modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies over the site. The
18	Čech approach developed here sidesteps the issue: it computes `Rⁱ f_* F` as the
19	cohomology of an explicit complex built from the pushforwards of `F` over the
20	finite intersections of an affine open cover, producing an **unconditional**
21	construction of `Rⁱ f_*` for quasi-coherent `F` and separated quasi-compact `f`.
22

import Mathlib

namespace CategoryTheory

variable {𝒜 : Type*} [Category 𝒜] [Abelian 𝒜] [HasInjectiveResolutions 𝒜]
variable {ℬ : Type*} [Category ℬ] [Abelian ℬ]

/-- An object `J` of `𝒜` is *right-`G`-acyclic* when the higher right-derived
functors of `G` vanish at `J`: `(Rᵏ⁺¹ G)(J) = 0` for all `k : ℕ`. -/
class Functor.IsRightAcyclic (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] (J : 𝒜) : Prop where
  vanish : ∀ k : ℕ, IsZero ((G.rightDerived (k + 1)).obj J)

instance (priority := 100) Functor.IsRightAcyclic.ofInjective
    (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] (J : 𝒜) [Injective J] : Functor.IsRightAcyclic G J where
  vanish k := Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ G k J

end CategoryTheory

import Mathlib

/-!
# Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors

This file provides the abstract homological-algebra core underlying the Čech
computation of higher direct images (Stacks Tag 015E, Leray's acyclicity lemma):
**an acyclic resolution computes every right-derived functor**.

Throughout, `𝒜` and `ℬ` are abelian categories, `𝒜` has injective resolutions
(so that `G.rightDerived n` is everywhere defined), and `G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ` is an
additive functor.

## Declarations

* `CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic` — typeclass for right-`G`-acyclic objects.
* Instance: every injective object is right-acyclic (from Mathlib's
  `Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ`).

The following declarations are outlined in the strategy block below and will be
constructed by the prover in `mathlib-build` mode:

* `CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact` — dual Horseshoe Lemma:
  lift `0 → A → B → C → 0` to a degreewise-split SES of injective resolutions.
* `CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic` — dimension-shift
  isomorphism `(Rᵏ G)(Z) ≅ (Rᵏ⁺¹ G)(A)` across a SES with acyclic middle term.
* `CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution` — main theorem:
  `(Rⁿ G)(A) ≅ Hⁿ(G(J•))` for any acyclic resolution `J•` of `A`.

See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_AcyclicResolution.tex` and
`.archon/analogies/p4-derived-les.md` for the full informal argument.

## Mathlib building blocks (all verified present)

All from `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Abelian/RightDerived.lean`:
- `CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj` — iso
  `(F.rightDerived n).obj X ≅ Hⁿ(F.mapHomologicalComplex.obj I.cocomplex)`.
- `CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf` — `R⁰G ≅ G` (left-exact `G`).
- `CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ` — vanishing on
  injectives: `IsZero ((F.rightDerived (n+1)).obj J)` for `[Injective J]`.

From `Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/HomologySequence.lean`:
- `CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁`
- `CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₂`
- `CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₃`
- `CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ`

## Source

- Tag 0157 (definition-derived-functor, items 3–4)
- Tag 015C (lemma-F-acyclic, part 2)
- Tag 015D (lemma-F-acyclic-ses)

namespace CategoryTheory

variable {𝒜 : Type*} [Category 𝒜] [Abelian 𝒜] [HasInjectiveResolutions 𝒜]
variable {ℬ : Type*} [Category ℬ] [Abelian ℬ]

/-!
### Right-acyclic objects
Blueprint: `def:right_acyclic` (§ "Right-acyclic objects").
-/

/-- An object `J : 𝒜` is *right-`G`-acyclic* when every higher right-derived
functor of `G` vanishes at `J`:
```
(Rᵏ⁺¹ G)(J) = 0   for all k : ℕ.
```
The index-shifted quantifier `k + 1` matches the statement of
`Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ` and avoids an inequality
side-condition; it is equivalent to `(Rⁿ G)(J) = 0` for all `n ≥ 1`.

Blueprint: `CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic` (`def:right_acyclic`).
-/
class Functor.IsRightAcyclic (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] (J : 𝒜) : Prop where
  vanish : ∀ k : ℕ, IsZero ((G.rightDerived (k + 1)).obj J)

/-- Every injective object is right-`G`-acyclic.
Follows immediately from `Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ`. -/
instance (priority := 100) Functor.IsRightAcyclic.ofInjective
    (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] (J : 𝒜) [Injective J] : Functor.IsRightAcyclic G J where
  vanish k := Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ G k J
